items.question: What does embedding visualization own vs t-SNE/UMAP topics?
items.answer: Embedding visualization owns plotting 2D/3D coordinates, metadata encodings (color, size, markers), interpretation guardrails, and publication practices. t-SNE and UMAP own the algorithms that produce coordinates—perplexity, n_neighbors, objectives, and tuning.

items.question: How should categorical labels be encoded on an embedding scatter?
items.answer: Map each category to a discrete color from a colorblind-safe qualitative palette, provide a clear legend, use moderate alpha for overlap, and facet or subsample when there are many categories—avoid sequential colormaps that imply ordering.

items.question: Why is overplotting dangerous when interpreting clusters?
items.answer: Saturated ink hides heterogeneity and outliers; dense regions look uniform and thin structures vanish, leading to over-confident cluster claims or missed error modes—mitigate with alpha, hexbin, or stratified subsampling.

items.question: How do embedding plots complement a confusion matrix?
items.answer: Confusion matrices quantify class-pair error rates; embedding plots show where those errors live spatially—color by true label and mark misclassifications to see if, e.g., 4-vs-9 errors colocate in a shared region of representation space.

items.question: What should a publication-ready embedding figure include beyond the scatter?
items.answer: Method name and key hyperparameters in the title or caption, axis labels, sample count and any subsampling note, accessible legend or colorbar, saved coordinates/seeds for reproducibility, and cautious language about non-linear distance distortion.
